operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Event boundary recognition in OpenCog Atomese representation issues (151e70039fc670ab)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Event boundary recognition in OpenCog Atomese representation issues
Messages: 5
Date range: 2015-12-28 to 2016-01-05
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 151e70039fc670ab

============================================================

--- Message 1/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-28

I have written thoughts about how to handle event boundary recognition using Atomese representations, GSNs, etc. This is an extension of my prior suggestions of how to wrap up deep learning algorithms in OpenCog.

--- Message 2/5 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-12-29

The event-detection problem reminds me of a different AI problem. Suppose you have football players on a field, and you want to figure out the play type from their movement tracks. The input is a collection of 2D tracks.

--- Message 3/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-30

The problem I was aiming to solve FIRST was not identifying or clustering events, but merely recognizing the natural beginnings and endings of events. It seems that event boundaries are characterized by discontinuity of predictability. I propose using multivariate permutation entropy.

--- Message 4/5 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-12-30

Segmentation is a necessary starting point, and permutation entropy sounds quite reasonable. I started a wiki page on Motion analysis. Re Atomese: the standard syntax for DefineSchema is DefineLink with DefinedSchema and Lambda.

--- Message 5/5 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller via opencog
Date: 2016-01-05

I am OK to use nullary DefinedSchemaNode as constant, and have DefineLink only work on DefinedSchemaNode, DefinedPredicateNode and DefinedTypeNode. The current type system over defined schema node is really cool!

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+FilterLink and maybe SignatureLink (15005d05f63837db)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: FilterLink and maybe SignatureLink
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-09-25 to 28 Sep 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 15005d05f63837db

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-25

Amen, et al, I wrote up a proposal for a FilterLink: http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/FilterLink

I was wondering if it might be useful for filtering out unwanted junk in the backward or forward chainers.

The idea arises naturally when one thinks about what might happen if one uses fancy type declarations (a SignatureLink) with a PutLink.

-- Linas

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 28 Sep 2015

Misgana has implemented the idea of the focus-set for the forward-chainer. The big plus about the nesting of rules (BindLink/GetLink/PutLink) is one doesn't have to remember the variable binding in the inner-nested rules.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Fwd Re Getting started -- re Linux Systems Programmer Position @ OpenCog (150960c875b65be8)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Fwd Re Getting started -- re Linux Systems Programmer Position @ OpenCog

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-10-23

Hi, Hedayat, I'm bringing that to the opencog list to hear others opinions. Is there any preference for indentation of class access level specifiers (private, protected, public)? Personally, I would favor no indentation because indentation and 80-columns lines don't mix well.

Nil

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-04

agree w/Nil. I usually have 6 or 8 windows open at once, and even on today's big, wide monitors, its hard to get them to fit well on the screen. So too much indentation makes it hard to work.

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Fwd Slack MOSES PSO discussion (14e769ab7988e0c3)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Fwd Slack MOSES PSO discussion
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-07-10 to 2015-07-11
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14e769ab7988e0c3

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-10

I was surprised to read you attempted PSO with discrete data. There is one thing you should know about hillclimbing: the amount of steps depends on the random seed. The variation can be huge. You cannot beat the average by launching N copies in parallel.

-linas

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-11

I am happy with ordinary hill-climbing for the discrete problems, and suggest not wasting any time there. However, PSO for the continuous-valued problems should be a huge, major win, and I am very excited by having that work. Almost anything would be better than hillclimbing with contin_t.

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Fwd Slack Notifications from the OpenCog team for Jul 3rd, 2015 at 830 pm (14e761788746ab50)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Fwd Slack Notifications from the OpenCog team for Jul 3rd, 2015 at 830 pm
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-07-10 to 2015-07-10
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14e761788746ab50

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-07-10

Apparently trying to answer these slack thingies by email causes the email to bounce. I have not yet figured out how to edit discussions on slack.

Anyway, has something been decided on the below? I'm not sure what the difference is between option 2 and 3: in either case, you have to evaluate a combo tree to find the function value.

In option 2, you seem to want to change float values in-place, but that won't work if you are running evaluations in multiple threads. Maybe you could make one copy of a combo tree per thread, and then alter the floats inside of it, in-place?

--linas

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Arley Ristar
Date: 2015-07-10

Forget about these. These were the options that I thought before I know that we could change the continuous representation. For now I'm trying to do the tests that Nil requested.

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Arley Ristar
Date: 2015-07-10

Apparently, Slack just notifies. There are options to integrate Slack and Gmail using outside services like IFTTT, but it should have this integration natively. Or you could try the IRC: https://opencog.slack.com/account/gateways

operations.content:

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-08

1) Re: slack: when I click on the slack link I get taken to messages/moses/ where the last message is from 3 August. None of the channels seem to contain the slack message below. Was the message deleted?

2) Not sure where you get the 'Linas hates the one-mainline approach'. I've never advocated more than one mainline.

--linas

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-09-09

That message is in the hk-scrum private group. 'Hate' obviously is too strong a word and was just used informally there, sorry... What I meant was that you seem not to like the approach I labeled 'APPROACH 1: SINGLE MAINLINE BRANCH' which involves having a single mainline 'master' branch and not breaking the unit tests thereon.

ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Getting started (14f68a1629a5b871)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Getting started
Messages: 2
Date range: 25 Aug 2015 to 2015-08-26
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f68a1629a5b871

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Param Hanji
Date: 25 Aug 2015

Hey guys, I'm a college student with a lot of free time. I've spent considerable time reading up and getting an idea of how all the components are supposed to work together. I now hope to focus my learning on a specific field.

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-08-26

Getting all the components to work together as the theory suggests they should, is one of the challenges we are facing.

In terms of learning: learn how to use the Pattern Matcher with the Atomspace, run the NLP comprehension pipeline, run MOSES, and run the PLN forward-chainer examples.

-- ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Google Summer of Code Projects page (14be57504e0b580e)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Google Summer of Code Projects page
Messages: 2
Date range: 2015-03-04 to 2015-03-05
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14be57504e0b580e

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-04

Hi all,

I have made a new, cleaned-up page of Google Summer of Code 2015 projects...
http://wiki.opencog.org/w/GSoC_2015_Project_Ideas

Mostly I imported stuff from the previous page, omitting stuff that's already been done or that seemed like suboptimal projects and adding a couple more things.

Experienced Opencoggers should feel free to add new projects to this new page, if they are willing to mentor them.

Thanks
Ben

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-03-05

Hi Ben, all,

I'm a bit ambivalent about the MOSES projects. The reason is because I suspect we're about to make some radical changes in MOSES (migrating to a more AtomSpace-ish environment) where a lot of functions are gonna take place in the atomspace, such as program reduction, type checking, defining sub-routines, etc.

In fact I'm wondering if we should not try to steer the projects towards that migration rather than extending MOSES.

I've added myself as possible (co-)mentor in the projects I believe I can be. I fear this year we're gonna have a mentor bottleneck.

Nil

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+GSoc2015 Neo4j Graph Backing Store (14cfe97897ee29d7)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: GSoc2015 Neo4j Graph Backing Store
Messages: 7
Date range: 2015-04-28 to 2015-05-05
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14cfe97897ee29d7

============================================================

--- Message 1/7 ---
From: Hendy Irawan
Date: 2015-04-28

Thank you for accepting me into GSoC 2015. I have been thinking of two approaches for the OpenCog Neo4jGraphStore mechanism: 1) Neo4j as daemon accessed via REST API, or 2) Neo4j embedded communicating via ZeroMQ. I currently favor approach #2.

--- Message 2/7 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 2015-04-29

What do you mean by OpenCog-Java app?

--- Message 3/7 ---
From: Hendy Irawan
Date: 2015-04-29

It would be a Neo4j Backing Store implemented in Java, communicating with OpenCog server via ZeroMQ.

--- Message 4/7 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 2015-05-04

Linas do you have any comments on Hendy suggestions?

--- Message 5/7 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-04

I know that Hendy is extremely comfortable with Java. Googling neo4j c++ reveals nothing, which leaves zeromq as a reasonable choice. Using zeromq does introduce latency and a choke-point.

--- Message 6/7 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-05-04

The current atomspace backingstore API is extremely simple. It does not require cypher at all. The proposal is: 1) implement current backing store API using ZMQ, 2) add a new call that passes a SatisfactionLink and converts it into a cypher query.

--- Message 7/7 ---
From: Hendy Irawan
Date: 2015-05-05

Yes, that is my plan. Neo4j can be deployed client-server or embedded. Cypher is like SQL, Neo4j can optimize the query. I also hope to structure performance and functional test suites.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+GSoc2015 Propose to work on Neo4j Backing Store - OpenCog (14bfd3c90629e681)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: GSoc2015 Propose to work on Neo4j Backing Store - OpenCog

Source: opencog@googlegroups.com

============================================================

--- Message 1/17 ---
From: Hendy Irawan
Date: 2015-03-09

Dear OpenCog members especially Dr. Ben Goertzel,

After looking at the current OpenCog project ideas I'm most interested in
working on Neo4j Backing Store as described in:

http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Neo4j_Backing_Store

I have some background experience working with Neo4j, in a social commerce
 [.. [...]

--- Message 2/17 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-09

Hi Hendy,

Sure, we would welcome a GSoC 2015 proposal for a Neo4j Backing Store
project.   Your experience with Neo4j would seem to make you qualified
to do such a project...

thanks
ben

--- Message 3/17 ---
From: Hendy Irawan
Date: 2015-03-09

Thank you Dr. Goertzel, I'll let you know of my progress with proposal.
Wish me luck :)

Best regards,
Hendy

--- Message 4/17 ---
From: Hendy Irawan
Date: 2015-03-09

Hi OpenCog / Dr. Ben Goertzel / Dr. Cosmo Harrigan,

I've curated the Neo4j Wiki page a bit especially on this section, please
see:

http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Neo4j_Backing_Store#Example_OpenCog_biology_query

While Neo4j is good for "manual" traversals, I believe Dr. Cosmo Harrigan's
 [...]

--- Message 5/17 ---
From: Hendy Irawan
Date: 2015-03-09

I wanted to mention that you can try the biology query example online at:
http://console.neo4j.org/r/wmrc6v

Regards,
Hendy

--- Message 6/17 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-10

Hendy,

This looks like a good approach to start with.   Transforming PM queries to
Cypher queries is straightforward and may often give good results.   If the
performance is poor with this approach in some cases, then we can design
special traversals to work around the problem.

--  Ben

p.s. BTW Cosmo Harrigan is not a Dr ---  though I supposeunder some
qualification he may perhaps qualify as a witch doctor '=)

--- Message 7/17 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-10

FWIW, I looked over the page.

The two example queries, one for bio, one for nlp, both seem to be against
databases that are small enough to fit in RAM. Thus, they can be performed
(fairly) efficiently in RAM today, without any modification to any code.
Also, because they're in-RAM, they would not really stress the neo4j query
optimizer: the hard part of querying is always figuring out how to access
the disk drive, not how to get to data that is in RAM.

The example bio query is actually almost too small and simple for the
pattern matcher: you'll surely get better performance if you don't use the
pattern matcher for it.  The pattern matcher does not do anything clever
for small, simple queries: it makes lots of worst-case assumptions. If you
 [...]

--- Message 8/17 ---
From: Hendy Irawan
Date: 2015-03-10

Thank you Dr. Vepstas for your assessment.

Yes I agree, the examples currently fairly simple.
My hunch is that Cypher should be good for many ue cases with acceptable
performance, especially for queries returning many data instead of small
 [...]

--- Message 9/17 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-10

Hendy,

Eddie or Amen (cc'd) can tell you where to get an Atomspace of biological
information which is reasonably large and complex...

ben

--- Message 10/17 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 10 Mar 2015

Hi Hendy,

check https://github.com/opencog/agi-bio/blob/master/knowledge-import/Bio_schemeFiles.zip
I am not sure if it is the a full port of the biolgical dataset, but you 
could use the scripts within the containing directory to generate the 
scheme-atomspace representation.

--- Message 11/17 ---
From: Hendy Irawan
Date: 2015-03-10

Thank you Amen, that's great!

Yup, a Scheme file (or generator) would be a good format for Atomspace dump.

Self-note: need to distinguish between "creating a new node" vs "linking to
an existing node(s)". Not sure yet whether this distinction is already
represented in the Scheme dumps.

 [...]

--- Message 12/17 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 10 Mar 2015

Check http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Scheme for some of the scheme utilities

--- Message 13/17 ---

I've looked at the data and it looks like a 212 MiB of full Scheme goodness

the proposed Neo4j graph backing store.

I plan to load this data into Neo4j by drafting a mapping/schema convention

About the Cypher translator, the idea is rather than coding the traversal

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Gsoc2016 (15156ecc9164b2be)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Gsoc2016
Messages: 5
Date range: 2015-11-29 to 2015-12-01
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 15156ecc9164b2be

============================================================

--- Message 1/5 ---
From: deepthimenon0461@gmail.com
Date: 2015-11-29

I would like to contribute to opencog. I have good c++, c and competitive programming skills. I have done some projects in ML and neural nets using caffe. Can someone help me get started?

--- Message 2/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-30

We do not yet know if OpenCog will be accepted for GSoC 2016, but anyone who has contributed already as a volunteer will tend to get to the front of the list. In late December I plan to better organize TODO tasks for newbies. One interesting project might be improvements to the Pattern Miner. There is also lots to do with NLP pipeline, PLN reasoning engine, and MOSES.

--- Message 3/5 ---
From: deepthimenon0461@gmail.com
Date: 2015-12-01

Thank you very much for responding Ben will try my best.

--- Message 4/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-01

Sure. If you have an interest in some specific project or aspect, just let us know on the email list, and we can give you some guidance in that direction.

--- Message 5/5 ---
From: Pheonix
Date: 2015-12-01

I am interested in machine learning and computer vision. I was thinking of making a better face detector/expression detector using neural nets or tensor flow.

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Marcos Pividori
Date: 2015-08-26

Hi everyone, I have been working this summer on the GSoC Project: Haskell bindings for OpenCog, mentored by Nil Geisweiller. I wrapped the C++ code in a C library and used FFI to interact from Haskell. [...]

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-08-27

Fantastic! This was a heroic GSoC effort I must say; this was not an easy thing to get done during the summer, as there were various conceptual issues to work out alongside lots of practical nitty-gritty stuff. I look forward to playing w/ this myself as time permits...

-- Ben

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-08-27

Thanks for your excellent work Marcos! Thanks for your patience during our lengthy hangouts to help me understand your work and improve my Haskell skills. The mentor-students roles have switched more than once in that project. ;-). But the result is what matters, a solid contribution.

Nil

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-04-28

Yi-Shan,

For the Minecraft projects that were accepted, the mentor is Lake Watkins, <nemquae@gmail.com>

It's generally better to keep discussions on this email list, or on the OpenCog Slack team which is about to be launched. Because for most projects there are many OpenCoggers besides the mentor who may have useful feedback.

-- Ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+GSoC 2015 - Natural Language Processing. (14c1d37b1c2daa8d)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: GSoC 2015 - Natural Language Processing.
Messages: 3
Date range: 15 Mar 2015 to 2015-03-15
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14c1d37b1c2daa8d

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Ashish Bedi
Date: 15 Mar 2015

Sir, This is Ashish Kumar Bedi, a 2nd year undergraduate CS student at IIT Mandi, India. I am interested in contributing to opencog in NLP, specifically implementing simplified Word grammar parsing in Atomspace.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-16

Hi Ashish,

Given your math background, I wonder if you might consider this project:
http://wiki.opencog.org/w/GSoC_2015_Project_Ideas#Implement_and_integrate_Imprecise_Probability_truth_value_math

Basically this involves some Monte Carlo integration coding, and would entail you learning about probabilistic logic and imprecise probabilities.

I'm just thinking you may not have the right linguistics background for the Word Grammar project.

-- Ben

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Ashish Bedi
Date: 2015-03-15

Sir, This is Ashish Kumar Bedi. I am interested in contributing to opencog in NLP project and specificaly in implementing simplified Word grammar parsing in Atomspace.
